## deepwiki
DeepWiki MCP provides AI-powered documentation for GitHub repositories.

Available tools:
- read_wiki_structure: Get a list of documentation topics for a repository
- read_wiki_contents: View full documentation about a repository
- ask_question: Ask any question about a repository and get an AI-powered answer
- list_available_repos: List your available repositories (private mode only)
- generate_wiki: Generate a codebase wiki for a repository — only use when explicitly requested by the user (private mode only)
- devin_knowledge_manage: Manage Devin knowledge notes and suggestions — list, search, get, create, update, delete notes, view folder structure, list/view/dismiss knowledge suggestions (private mode only)
- devin_playbook_manage: Manage Devin playbooks — list, get, create, update, delete (private mode only)
- devin_schedule_manage: Manage scheduled Devin sessions — list, get, create, update, delete (private mode only)
- devin_session_create: Create one or more child Devin sessions (private mode only)
- devin_session_interact: Manage a Devin session — get status, send messages, sleep/terminate/archive, read messages & attachments, manage tags (private mode only)
- devin_session_events: Inspect session events — list summaries, fetch full details, or search event contents (private mode only)
- devin_session_search: Search and filter Devin sessions (private mode only)
- list_integrations: List all native integrations and MCP servers with their status and settings URLs (private mode only)

## plugin:qmd-plugin:qmd
QMD is your local search engine over 34 markdown documents.

Collections (scope with `collection` parameter):
  - "electrobun-python-sidecar-extraction" (8 docs)
  - "my-electrobun-extraction" (5 docs)
  - "my-electrobun-docs" (11 docs)
  - "my-electrobun-temporal" (4 docs)
  - "electrobun-python-sidecar-temporal" (4 docs)
  - "electrobun-python-sidecar-brief" (1 docs)
  - "my-electrobun-brief" (1 docs)

Search: Use `query` with sub-queries (lex/vec/hyde):
  - type:'lex' — BM25 keyword search (exact terms, fast)
  - type:'vec' — semantic vector search (meaning-based)
  - type:'hyde' — hypothetical document (write what the answer looks like)

  Always provide `intent` on every search call to disambiguate and improve snippets.

Examples:
  Quick keyword lookup: [{type:'lex', query:'error handling'}]
  Semantic search: [{type:'vec', query:'how to handle errors gracefully'}]
  Best results: [{type:'lex', query:'error'}, {type:'vec', query:'error handling best practices'}]
  With intent: searches=[{type:'lex', query:'performance'}], intent='web page load times'

Retrieval:
  - `get` — single document by path or docid (#abc123). Supports line offset (`file.md:100`).
  - `multi_get` — batch retrieve by glob (`journals/2025-05*.md`) or comma-separated list.

Tips:
  - File paths in results are relative to their collection.
  - Use `minScore: 0.5` to filter low-confidence results.
  - Results include a `context` field describing the content type.

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (triggers) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/triggers.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- find-skills: Helps users discover and install agent skills when they ask questions like "how do I do X", "find a skill for X", "is there a skill that can...", or express interest in extending capabilities. This skill should be used when the user is looking for functionality that might exist as an installable skill.
- remotion-best-practices: Best practices for Remotion - Video creation in React
- ccc: This skill should be used when code search is needed (whether explicitly requested or as part of completing a task), when indexing the codebase after changes, or when the user asks about ccc, cocoindex-code, or the codebase index. Trigger phrases include 'search the codebase', 'find code related to', 'update the index', 'ccc', 'cocoindex-code'.
- vercel-react-best-practices: React and Next.js performance optimization guidelines from Vercel Engineering. This skill should be used when writing, reviewing, or refactoring React/Next.js code to ensure optimal performance patterns. Triggers on tasks involving React components, Next.js pages, data fetching, bundle optimization, or performance improvements.
- ast-grep: Guide for writing ast-grep rules to perform structural code search and analysis. Use when users need to search codebases using Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) patterns, find specific code structures, or perform complex code queries that go beyond simple text search. This skill should be used when users ask to search for code patterns, find specific language constructs, or locate code with particular structural characteristics.
- firecrawl: Official Firecrawl CLI skill for web scraping, search, crawling, and browser automation. Returns clean LLM-optimized markdown.

USE FOR:
- Web search and research
- Scraping pages, docs, and articles
- Site mapping and bulk content extraction
- Browser automation for interactive pages

Must be pre-installed and authenticated. See rules/install.md for setup, rules/security.md for output handling.
- baton-project-setup: Configure Baton workspace setup for this project
- bmad-editorial-review-prose: Clinical copy-editor that reviews text for communication issues. Use when user says review for prose or improve the prose
- bmad-product-brief: Create or update product briefs through guided or autonomous discovery. Use when the user requests to create or update a Product Brief.
- bmad-prfaq: Working Backwards PRFAQ challenge to forge product concepts. Use when the user requests to 'create a PRFAQ', 'work backwards', or 'run the PRFAQ challenge'.
- bmad-code-review: Review code changes adversarially using parallel review layers (Blind Hunter, Edge Case Hunter, Acceptance Auditor) with structured triage into actionable categories. Use when the user says "run code review" or "review this code"
- bmad-domain-research: Conduct domain and industry research. Use when the user says wants to do domain research for a topic or industry
- bmad-index-docs: Generates or updates an index.md to reference all docs in the folder. Use if user requests to create or update an index of all files in a specific folder
- bmad-agent-builder: Builds, edits or analyzes Agent Skills through conversational discovery. Use when the user requests to "Create an Agent", "Analyze an Agent" or "Edit an Agent".
- bmad-create-epics-and-stories: Break requirements into epics and user stories. Use when the user says "create the epics and stories list"
- bmad-technical-research: Conduct technical research on technologies and architecture. Use when the user says they would like to do or produce a technical research report
- bmad-create-prd: Create a PRD from scratch. Use when the user says "lets create a product requirements document" or "I want to create a new PRD"
- bmad-generate-project-context: Create project-context.md with AI rules. Use when the user says "generate project context" or "create project context"
- bmad-agent-pm: Product manager for PRD creation and requirements discovery. Use when the user asks to talk to John or requests the product manager.
- bmad-party-mode: Orchestrates group discussions between installed BMAD agents, enabling natural multi-agent conversations where each agent is a real subagent with independent thinking. Use when user requests party mode, wants multiple agent perspectives, group discussion, roundtable, or multi-agent conversation about their project.
- bmad-agent-dev: Senior software engineer for story execution and code implementation. Use when the user asks to talk to Amelia or requests the developer agent.
- bmad-agent-analyst: Strategic business analyst and requirements expert. Use when the user asks to talk to Mary or requests the business analyst.
- bmad-advanced-elicitation: Push the LLM to reconsider, refine, and improve its recent output. Use when user asks for deeper critique or mentions a known deeper critique method, e.g. socratic, first principles, pre-mortem, red team.
- bmad-qa-generate-e2e-tests: Generate end to end automated tests for existing features. Use when the user says "create qa automated tests for [feature]"
- bmad-cis-agent-innovation-strategist: Disruptive innovation oracle for business model innovation and strategic disruption. Use when the user asks to talk to Victor or requests the Disruptive Innovation Oracle.
- bmad-sprint-planning: Generate sprint status tracking from epics. Use when the user says "run sprint planning" or "generate sprint plan"
- bmad-cis-problem-solving: Apply systematic problem-solving methodologies to complex challenges. Use when the user says "guide me through structured problem solving" or "I want to crack this challenge with guided problem solving techniques"
- bmad-cis-agent-brainstorming-coach: Elite brainstorming specialist for facilitated ideation sessions. Use when the user asks to talk to Carson or requests the Brainstorming Specialist.
- bmad-agent-ux-designer: UX designer and UI specialist. Use when the user asks to talk to Sally or requests the UX designer.
- bmad-correct-course: Manage significant changes during sprint execution. Use when the user says "correct course" or "propose sprint change"
- bmad-workflow-builder: Builds, converts, and analyzes workflows and skills. Use when the user requests to "build a workflow", "modify a workflow", "quality check workflow", "analyze skill", or "convert a skill".
- bmad-distillator: Lossless LLM-optimized compression of source documents. Use when the user requests to 'distill documents' or 'create a distillate'.
- bmad-shard-doc: Splits large markdown documents into smaller, organized files based on level 2 (default) sections. Use if the user says perform shard document
- bmad-cis-agent-design-thinking-coach: Design thinking maestro for human-centered design processes. Use when the user asks to talk to Maya or requests the Design Thinking Maestro.
- bmad-brainstorming: Facilitate interactive brainstorming sessions using diverse creative techniques and ideation methods. Use when the user says help me brainstorm or help me ideate.
- bmad-checkpoint-preview: LLM-assisted human-in-the-loop review. Make sense of a change, focus attention where it matters, test. Use when the user says "checkpoint", "human review", or "walk me through this change".
- bmad-market-research: Conduct market research on competition and customers. Use when the user says they need market research
- bmad-edit-prd: Edit an existing PRD. Use when the user says "edit this PRD".
- bmad-bmb-setup: Sets up BMad Builder module in a project. Use when the user requests to 'install bmb module', 'configure BMad Builder', or 'setup BMad Builder'.
- bmad-cis-agent-presentation-master: Visual communication and presentation expert for slide decks, pitch decks, and visual storytelling. Use when the user asks to talk to Caravaggio or requests the Presentation Expert.
- bmad-cis-agent-creative-problem-solver: Master problem solver for systematic problem-solving methodologies. Use when the user asks to talk to Dr. Quinn or requests the Master Problem Solver.
- bmad-editorial-review-structure: Structural editor that proposes cuts, reorganization, and simplification while preserving comprehension. Use when user requests structural review or editorial review of structure
- bmad-help: Analyzes current state and user query to answer BMad questions or recommend the next skill(s) to use. Use when user asks for help, bmad help, what to do next, or what to start with in BMad.
- bmad-cis-design-thinking: Guide human-centered design processes using empathy-driven methodologies. Use when the user says "lets run design thinking" or "I want to apply design thinking"
- bmad-review-edge-case-hunter: Walk every branching path and boundary condition in content, report only unhandled edge cases. Orthogonal to adversarial review - method-driven not attitude-driven. Use when you need exhaustive edge-case analysis of code, specs, or diffs.
- bmad-cis-agent-storyteller: Master storyteller for compelling narratives using proven frameworks. Use when the user asks to talk to Sophia or requests the Master Storyteller.
- bmad-quick-dev: Implements any user intent, requirement, story, bug fix or change request by producing clean working code artifacts that follow the project's existing architecture, patterns and conventions. Use when the user wants to build, fix, tweak, refactor, add or modify any code, component or feature.
- bmad-retrospective: Post-epic review to extract lessons and assess success. Use when the user says "run a retrospective" or "lets retro the epic [epic]"
- bmad-agent-architect: System architect and technical design leader. Use when the user asks to talk to Winston or requests the architect.
- bmad-cis-innovation-strategy: Identify disruption opportunities and architect business model innovation. Use when the user says "lets create an innovation strategy" or "I want to find disruption opportunities"
- bmad-document-project: Document brownfield projects for AI context. Use when the user says "document this project" or "generate project docs"
- bmad-create-story: Creates a dedicated story file with all the context the agent will need to implement it later. Use when the user says "create the next story" or "create story [story identifier]"
- bmad-check-implementation-readiness: Validate PRD, UX, Architecture and Epics specs are complete. Use when the user says "check implementation readiness".
- bmad-create-architecture: Create architecture solution design decisions for AI agent consistency. Use when the user says "lets create architecture" or "create technical architecture" or "create a solution design"
- bmad-validate-prd: Validate a PRD against standards. Use when the user says "validate this PRD" or "run PRD validation"
- bmad-create-ux-design: Plan UX patterns and design specifications. Use when the user says "lets create UX design" or "create UX specifications" or "help me plan the UX"
- bmad-dev-story: Execute story implementation following a context filled story spec file. Use when the user says "dev this story [story file]" or "implement the next story in the sprint plan"
- bmad-cis-storytelling: Craft compelling narratives using story frameworks. Use when the user says "help me with storytelling" or "I want to create a narrative through storytelling"
- bmad-sprint-status: Summarize sprint status and surface risks. Use when the user says "check sprint status" or "show sprint status"
- bmad-agent-tech-writer: Technical documentation specialist and knowledge curator. Use when the user asks to talk to Paige or requests the tech writer.
- bmad-review-adversarial-general: Perform a Cynical Review and produce a findings report. Use when the user requests a critical review of something
- bmad-module-builder: Plans, creates, and validates BMad modules. Use when the user requests to 'ideate module', 'plan a module', 'create module', 'build a module', or 'validate module'.
- feature-dev:feature-dev: Guided feature development with codebase understanding and architecture focus
- qmd-plugin:qmd: Search personal markdown knowledge bases, notes, meeting transcripts, and documentation using QMD - a local hybrid search engine. Combines BM25 keyword search, vector semantic search, and LLM re-ranking. Use when users ask to search notes, find documents, look up information in their knowledge base, retrieve meeting notes, or search documentation. Triggers on "search markdown files", "search my notes", "find in docs", "look up", "what did I write about", "meeting notes about".
- claude-mem:make-plan: Create a detailed, phased implementation plan with documentation discovery. Use when asked to plan a feature, task, or multi-step implementation — especially before executing with do.
- claude-mem:do: Execute a phased implementation plan using subagents. Use when asked to execute, run, or carry out a plan — especially one created by make-plan.
- claude-mem:mem-search: Search claude-mem's persistent cross-session memory database. Use when user asks "did we already solve this?", "how did we do X last time?", or needs work from previous sessions.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

file.content:
---
main_config: '{project-root}/_bmad/bmm/config.yaml'
---

# Code Review Workflow

**Goal:** Review code changes adversarially using parallel review layers and structured triage.

**Your Role:** You are an elite code reviewer. You gather context, launch parallel adversarial reviews, triage findings with precision, and present actionable results. No noise, no filler.


## WORKFLOW ARCHITECTURE

This uses **step-file architecture** for disciplined execution:

- **Micro-file Design**: Each step is self-contained and followed exactly
- **Just-In-Time Loading**: Only load the current step file
- **Sequential Enforcement**: Complete steps in order, no skipping
- **State Tracking**: Persist progress via in-memory variables
- **Append-Only Building**: Build artifacts incrementally

### Step Processing Rules

1. **READ COMPLETELY**: Read the entire step file before acting
2. **FOLLOW SEQUENCE**: Execute sections in order
3. **WAIT FOR INPUT**: Halt at checkpoints and wait for human
4. **LOAD NEXT**: When directed, read fully and follow the next step file

### Critical Rules (NO EXCEPTIONS)

- **NEVER** load multiple step files simultaneously
- **ALWAYS** read entire step file before execution
- **NEVER** skip steps or optimize the sequence
- **ALWAYS** follow the exact instructions in the step file
- **ALWAYS** halt at checkpoints and wait for human input


## INITIALIZATION SEQUENCE

### 1. Configuration Loading

Load and read full config from `{main_config}` and resolve:

- `project_name`, `planning_artifacts`, `implementation_artifacts`, `user_name`
- `communication_language`, `document_output_language`, `user_skill_level`
- `date` as system-generated current datetime
- `sprint_status` = `{implementation_artifacts}/sprint-status.yaml`
- `project_context` = `**/project-context.md` (load if exists)
- CLAUDE.md / memory files (load if exist)

YOU MUST ALWAYS SPEAK OUTPUT in your Agent communication style with the config `{communication_language}`.

### 2. First Step Execution

Read fully and follow: `./steps/step-01-gather-context.md` to begin the workflow.

content.content:
1	---
2	diff_output: '' # set at runtime
3	spec_file: '' # set at runtime (path or empty)
4	review_mode: '' # set at runtime: "full" or "no-spec"
5	story_key: '' # set at runtime when discovered from sprint status
6	---
7	
8	# Step 1: Gather Context
9	
10	## RULES
11	
12	- YOU MUST ALWAYS SPEAK OUTPUT in your Agent communication style with the config `{communication_language}`
13	- The prompt that triggered this workflow IS the intent — not a hint.
14	- Do not modify any files. This step is read-only.
15	
16	## INSTRUCTIONS
17	
18	1. **Find the review target.** The conversation context before this skill was triggered IS your starting point — not a blank slate. Check in this order — stop as soon as the review target is identified:
19	
20	   **Tier 1 — Explicit argument.**
21	   Did the user pass a PR, commit SHA, branch, spec file, or diff source this message?
22	   - PR reference → resolve to branch/commit via `gh pr view`. If resolution fails, ask for a SHA or branch.
23	   - Commit or branch → use directly.
24	   - Spec file → set `{spec_file}` to the provided path. Check its frontmatter for `baseline_commit`. If found, use as diff baseline. If not found, continue the cascade (a spec alone does not identify a diff source).
25	   - Also scan the argument for diff-mode keywords that narrow the scope:
26	     - "staged" / "staged changes" → Staged changes only
27	     - "uncommitted" / "working tree" / "all changes" → Uncommitted changes (staged + unstaged)
28	     - "branch diff" / "vs main" / "against main" / "compared to <branch>" → Branch diff (extract base branch if mentioned)
29	     - "commit range" / "last N commits" / "<from-sha>..<to-sha>" → Specific commit range
30	     - "this diff" / "provided diff" / "paste" → User-provided diff (do not match bare "diff" — it appears in other modes)
31	   - When multiple keywords match, prefer the most specific (e.g., "branch diff" over bare "diff").
32	
33	   **Tier 2 — Recent conversation.**
34	   Do the last few messages reveal what the user wants to be reviewed? Look for spec paths, commit refs, branches, PRs, or descriptions of a change. Apply the same diff-mode keyword scan and routing as Tier 1.
35	
36	   **Tier 3 — Sprint tracking.**
37	   Look for a sprint status file (`*sprint-status*`) in `{implementation_artifacts}` or `{planning_artifacts}`. If found, scan for stories with status `review`:
38	   - **Exactly one `review` story:** Set `{story_key}` to the story's key (e.g., `1-2-user-auth`). Suggest it: "I found story <story-id> in `review` status. Would you like to review its changes? [Y] Yes / [N] No, let me choose". If confirmed, use the story context to determine the diff source (branch name derived from story slug, or uncommitted changes). If declined, clear `{story_key}` and fall through.
39	   - **Multiple `review` stories:** Present them as numbered options alongside a manual choice option. Wait for user selection. If a story is selected, set `{story_key}` and use its context to determine the diff source. If manual choice is selected, clear `{story_key}` and fall through.
40	   - **None:** Fall through.
41	
42	   **Tier 4 — Current git state.**
43	   If version control is unavailable, skip to Tier 5. Otherwise, check the current branch and HEAD. If the branch is not `main` (or the default branch), confirm: "I see HEAD is `<short-sha>` on `<branch>` — do you want to review this branch's changes?" If confirmed, treat as a branch diff against `main`. If declined, fall through.
44	
45	   **Tier 5 — Ask.**
46	   Fall through to instruction 2.
47	
48	   Never ask extra questions beyond what the cascade prescribes. If a tier above already identified the target, skip the remaining tiers and proceed to instruction 3 (construct diff).
49	
50	2. HALT. Ask the user: **What do you want to review?** Present these options:
51	   - **Uncommitted changes** (staged + unstaged)
52	   - **Staged changes only**
53	   - **Branch diff** vs a base branch (ask which base branch)
54	   - **Specific commit range** (ask for the range)
55	   - **Provided diff or file list** (user pastes or provides a path)
56	
57	3. Construct `{diff_output}` from the chosen source.
58	   - For **staged changes only**: run `git diff --cached`.
59	   - For **uncommitted changes** (staged + unstaged): run `git diff HEAD`.
60	   - For **branch diff**: verify the base branch exists before running `git diff`. If it does not exist, HALT and ask the user for a valid branch.
61	   - For **commit range**: verify the range resolves. If it does not, HALT and ask the user for a valid range.
62	   - For **provided diff**: validate the content is non-empty and parseable as a unified diff. If it is not parseable, HALT and ask the user to provide a valid diff.
63	   - For **file list**: validate each path exists in the working tree. Construct `{diff_output}` by running `git diff HEAD -- <path1> <path2> ...`. If any paths are untracked (new files not yet staged), use `git diff --no-index /dev/null <path>` to include them. If the diff is empty (files have no uncommitted changes and are not untracked), ask the user whether to review the full file contents or to specify a different baseline.
64	   - After constructing `{diff_output}`, verify it is non-empty regardless of source type. If empty, HALT and tell the user there is nothing to review.
65	
66	4. **Set the spec context.**
67	   - If `{spec_file}` is already set (from Tier 1 or Tier 2): verify the file exists and is readable, then set `{review_mode}` = `"full"`.
68	   - Otherwise, ask the user: **Is there a spec or story file that provides context for these changes?**
69	     - If yes: set `{spec_file}` to the path provided, verify the file exists and is readable, then set `{review_mode}` = `"full"`.
70	     - If no: set `{review_mode}` = `"no-spec"`.
71	
72	5. If `{review_mode}` = `"full"` and the file at `{spec_file}` has a `context` field in its frontmatter listing additional docs, load each referenced document. Warn the user about any docs that cannot be found.
73	
74	6. Sanity check: if `{diff_output}` exceeds approximately 3000 lines, warn the user and offer to chunk the review by file group.
75	   - If the user opts to chunk: agree on the first group, narrow `{diff_output}` accordingly, and list the remaining groups for the user to note for follow-up runs.
76	   - If the user declines: proceed as-is with the full diff.
77	
78	### CHECKPOINT
79	
80	Present a summary before proceeding: diff stats (files changed, lines added/removed), `{review_mode}`, and loaded spec/context docs (if any). HALT and wait for user confirmation to proceed.
81	
82	
83	## NEXT
84	
85	Read fully and follow `./step-02-review.md`
86

---
diff_output: '' # set at runtime
spec_file: '' # set at runtime (path or empty)
review_mode: '' # set at runtime: "full" or "no-spec"
story_key: '' # set at runtime when discovered from sprint status
---

# Step 1: Gather Context

## RULES

- YOU MUST ALWAYS SPEAK OUTPUT in your Agent communication style with the config `{communication_language}`
- The prompt that triggered this workflow IS the intent — not a hint.
- Do not modify any files. This step is read-only.

## INSTRUCTIONS

1. **Find the review target.** The conversation context before this skill was triggered IS your starting point — not a blank slate. Check in this order — stop as soon as the review target is identified:

   **Tier 1 — Explicit argument.**
   Did the user pass a PR, commit SHA, branch, spec file, or diff source this message?
   - PR reference → resolve to branch/commit via `gh pr view`. If resolution fails, ask for a SHA or branch.
   - Commit or branch → use directly.
   - Spec file → set `{spec_file}` to the provided path. Check its frontmatter for `baseline_commit`. If found, use as diff baseline. If not found, continue the cascade (a spec alone does not identify a diff source).
   - Also scan the argument for diff-mode keywords that narrow the scope:
     - "staged" / "staged changes" → Staged changes only
     - "uncommitted" / "working tree" / "all changes" → Uncommitted changes (staged + unstaged)
     - "branch diff" / "vs main" / "against main" / "compared to <branch>" → Branch diff (extract base branch if mentioned)
     - "commit range" / "last N commits" / "<from-sha>..<to-sha>" → Specific commit range
     - "this diff" / "provided diff" / "paste" → User-provided diff (do not match bare "diff" — it appears in other modes)
   - When multiple keywords match, prefer the most specific (e.g., "branch diff" over bare "diff").

   **Tier 2 — Recent conversation.**
   Do the last few messages reveal what the user wants to be reviewed? Look for spec paths, commit refs, branches, PRs, or descriptions of a change. Apply the same diff-mode keyword scan and routing as Tier 1.

   **Tier 3 — Sprint tracking.**
   Look for a sprint status file (`*sprint-status*`) in `{implementation_artifacts}` or `{planning_artifacts}`. If found, scan for stories with status `review`:
   - **Exactly one `review` story:** Set `{story_key}` to the story's key (e.g., `1-2-user-auth`). Suggest it: "I found story <story-id> in `review` status. Would you like to review its changes? [Y] Yes / [N] No, let me choose". If confirmed, use the story context to determine the diff source (branch name derived from story slug, or uncommitted changes). If declined, clear `{story_key}` and fall through.
   - **Multiple `review` stories:** Present them as numbered options alongside a manual choice option. Wait for user selection. If a story is selected, set `{story_key}` and use its context to determine the diff source. If manual choice is selected, clear `{story_key}` and fall through.
   - **None:** Fall through.

   **Tier 4 — Current git state.**
   If version control is unavailable, skip to Tier 5. Otherwise, check the current branch and HEAD. If the branch is not `main` (or the default branch), confirm: "I see HEAD is `<short-sha>` on `<branch>` — do you want to review this branch's changes?" If confirmed, treat as a branch diff against `main`. If declined, fall through.

   **Tier 5 — Ask.**
   Fall through to instruction 2.

   Never ask extra questions beyond what the cascade prescribes. If a tier above already identified the target, skip the remaining tiers and proceed to instruction 3 (construct diff).

2. HALT. Ask the user: **What do you want to review?** Present these options:
   - **Uncommitted changes** (staged + unstaged)
   - **Staged changes only**
   - **Branch diff** vs a base branch (ask which base branch)
   - **Specific commit range** (ask for the range)
   - **Provided diff or file list** (user pastes or provides a path)

3. Construct `{diff_output}` from the chosen source.
   - For **staged changes only**: run `git diff --cached`.
   - For **uncommitted changes** (staged + unstaged): run `git diff HEAD`.
   - For **branch diff**: verify the base branch exists before running `git diff`. If it does not exist, HALT and ask the user for a valid branch.
   - For **commit range**: verify the range resolves. If it does not, HALT and ask the user for a valid range.
   - For **provided diff**: validate the content is non-empty and parseable as a unified diff. If it is not parseable, HALT and ask the user to provide a valid diff.
   - For **file list**: validate each path exists in the working tree. Construct `{diff_output}` by running `git diff HEAD -- <path1> <path2> ...`. If any paths are untracked (new files not yet staged), use `git diff --no-index /dev/null <path>` to include them. If the diff is empty (files have no uncommitted changes and are not untracked), ask the user whether to review the full file contents or to specify a different baseline.
   - After constructing `{diff_output}`, verify it is non-empty regardless of source type. If empty, HALT and tell the user there is nothing to review.

4. **Set the spec context.**
   - If `{spec_file}` is already set (from Tier 1 or Tier 2): verify the file exists and is readable, then set `{review_mode}` = `"full"`.
   - Otherwise, ask the user: **Is there a spec or story file that provides context for these changes?**
     - If yes: set `{spec_file}` to the path provided, verify the file exists and is readable, then set `{review_mode}` = `"full"`.
     - If no: set `{review_mode}` = `"no-spec"`.

5. If `{review_mode}` = `"full"` and the file at `{spec_file}` has a `context` field in its frontmatter listing additional docs, load each referenced document. Warn the user about any docs that cannot be found.

6. Sanity check: if `{diff_output}` exceeds approximately 3000 lines, warn the user and offer to chunk the review by file group.
   - If the user opts to chunk: agree on the first group, narrow `{diff_output}` accordingly, and list the remaining groups for the user to note for follow-up runs.
   - If the user declines: proceed as-is with the full diff.

### CHECKPOINT

Present a summary before proceeding: diff stats (files changed, lines added/removed), `{review_mode}`, and loaded spec/context docs (if any). HALT and wait for user confirmation to proceed.


## NEXT

Read fully and follow `./step-02-review.md`

8	# STATUS DEFINITIONS:
9	# ==================
10	# Epic Status:
11	#   - backlog: Epic not yet started
12	#   - in-progress: Epic actively being worked on
13	#   - done: All stories in epic completed
14	#
15	# Epic Status Transitions:

17	#   - in-progress → done: Manually when all stories reach 'done' status

content.content:
1	# Story 2.2: Seed initial `CHANGELOG.md` from existing git tags
2	
3	Status: review
4	
5	<!-- Note: Validation is optional. Run validate-create-story for quality check before dev-story. -->
6	
7	## Story
8	
9	As a downstream user pinning Skill Forge as a dependency,
10	I want a repo-resident `CHANGELOG.md` following Keep-a-Changelog v1.1.0 format with one versioned entry per published tag from `v0.3.0` through `v0.10.0`,
11	so that I can audit what changed in each past version without leaving the repo, the v1.0.0 release has a pre-existing changelog history to append to, and Story 3.1's `release.yaml` has a real seed file against which `npx conventional-changelog-cli -p conventionalcommits -i CHANGELOG.md -s` can append the next release entry.
12	
13	## Acceptance Criteria
14	
15	1. The CHANGELOG seed SHALL be produced from `npx conventional-changelog-cli -p conventionalcommits -r 0` (all-releases mode) — see Dev Notes § "CHANGELOG reconciliation decision" for the three reconciliation paths Story 2.1 flagged and the pre-condition that resolves the "Given no `CHANGELOG.md` exists" framing of the original AC.
16	2. The resulting `CHANGELOG.md` SHALL exist at the repo root and SHALL follow Keep-a-Changelog v1.1.0 shape: a top-level `# Changelog` header, an "All notable changes…" preamble line, then one `## [X.Y.Z] - YYYY-MM-DD` versioned section per published tag from `v0.3.0` through `v0.10.0` (16 sections total — see Dev Notes § "Live-state recon" for the exact tag list), each containing one or more of the Keep-a-Changelog category headings (`### Added`, `### Changed`, `### Deprecated`, `### Removed`, `### Fixed`, `### Security`) as the conventionalcommits preset emits them.
17	3. Tags newer than the most recent published version (none currently — `v0.10.0` is HEAD's most recent tag) SHALL NOT have a section. An "Unreleased" section MAY appear at the top if the tool emits one for commits since `v0.10.0`; if it does, the `Unreleased` heading SHALL be left in place (it's the standard Keep-a-Changelog hook for accumulating un-tagged work; Story 3.1 will replace it on the next release cut).
18	4. The pre-existing hand-written `## [1.0.0] - 2026-04-13` prose at the repo root SHALL NOT survive into the seeded `CHANGELOG.md`. The prose SHALL be preserved at `_bmad-output/planning-artifacts/v1-0-0-changelog-draft.md` (workshop file) so Story 5.1's pre-flight audit can pull the curated wording back when v1.0.0 is actually being cut. The workshop file SHALL include a one-line header explaining its purpose and pointing to Story 5.1 as the consumer.
19	5. The seed output SHALL be hand-reviewed for noise (commits incorrectly classified by the conventionalcommits preset, missing scopes, accidentally-shipped sentinel commits) and any corrections SHALL be applied directly to the file. AC #2 of the original epics.md formulation ("subsequent Epic 3 runs SHALL append new entries without overwriting existing prose") is satisfied by `conventional-changelog-cli -i ... -s`'s "stop at the first existing version heading" behavior — Story 3.1's append invocation will not touch the seeded prose, so hand-edits stick.
20	6. `README.md` SHALL gain a "Changelog" link pointing to `CHANGELOG.md`, placed adjacent to the existing "Release Process" subsection (lines 178-180 of README.md). The link SHALL be a one-sentence pointer ("Past releases are documented in [CHANGELOG.md](CHANGELOG.md).") — not a badge, not a duplicated table of versions. Discoverability is the goal; not duplicating the changelog into the README.
21	7. `npm test` (the full quality suite — `test:schemas`, `test:install`, `test:cli`, `test:workflow`, `test:python`, `test:knowledge`, `validate:schemas`, `validate:skills`, `validate:refs`, `lint`, `lint:md`, `format:check`) SHALL pass on the resulting branch. In particular `lint:md` SHALL pass on the new `CHANGELOG.md` file — see Dev Notes § "Markdownlint regression surface" for likely fix patterns if the seeded output trips a rule.
22	8. No file outside the seven below SHALL be modified by this story: `CHANGELOG.md` (replaced), `README.md` (one-link addition), `_bmad-output/planning-artifacts/v1-0-0-changelog-draft.md` (new workshop file). No changes to `package.json`, `package-lock.json`, `.github/workflows/*`, `docs/RELEASING.md`, `docs/STABILITY.md` (not yet authored — Story 2.3), or any source/test file. If the seeded file requires a markdownlint config tweak (e.g., extending an `ignores`/`MD024` rule to handle Keep-a-Changelog's repeated category headings), that tweak IS in scope and the touched config file is the eighth allowed file — see Dev Notes § "Markdownlint regression surface" for the expected fix path.
23	9. The commit SHALL use conventional-commit form. Suggested subject: `docs(changelog): seed initial CHANGELOG.md from existing tags v0.3.0..v0.10.0`. The body SHALL record (i) the reconciliation path chosen (recommended: Path 3 per Dev Notes § "CHANGELOG reconciliation decision") and the rationale, (ii) the exact `conventional-changelog-cli` invocation used (flags + version), (iii) one-line confirmation `npm test` passed post-seed, (iv) one-line note that the hand-written `[1.0.0]` prose was preserved to `_bmad-output/planning-artifacts/v1-0-0-changelog-draft.md` for Story 5.1, (v) one-line restatement that no `package.json` / `package-lock.json` / workflow changes piggybacked.
24	10. The deprecation warning Story 2.1 surfaced (`npm warn deprecated conventional-changelog-cli@5.0.0: This package is no longer maintained.`) SHALL appear during this story's seed invocation as well — that is expected and NOT a blocker. The warning is registry-side; tool execution remains correct. Do NOT switch tools to silence it (the choice between staying on `conventional-changelog-cli@5` versus migrating to `conventional-changelog` versus `git-cliff` is a Story 3.1 design-phase decision, captured in `_bmad-output/implementation-artifacts/deferred-work.md` § "Story 3.1 tooling decision").
25	
26	## Tasks / Subtasks
27	
28	- [x] **Task 1 — Live-state recon** (Epic 1 retrospective carry-forward; informs every later task)
29	  - [x] `git tag -l --sort=-creatordate` — confirm 16 tags `v0.10.0` → `v0.3.0` exist (no v0.1.x, no v0.2.x). If the count or shape differs from the recon snapshot in Dev Notes § "Live-state recon", capture the live state in Completion Notes and adjust expectations
30	  - [x] `wc -c CHANGELOG.md` and `git log -1 --format='%H %ad' -- CHANGELOG.md` — confirm the file is still the 3148-byte hand-written 2026-04-13 v1.0.0 draft Story 2.1 left untouched. If a different shape is on disk (e.g. someone else seeded between Story 2.1 and now), STOP and reconcile out-of-band before proceeding
31	  - [x] `npx --no-install conventional-changelog-cli --version` — confirm the binary resolves locally (Story 2.1's lockfile carries `conventional-changelog-cli@5.0.0`); expected output `5.0.0`
32	  - [x] `npx --no-install conventional-changelog-cli --help | head -40` — re-read the flag surface fresh. AC #1 mandates `-r 0`; the greenfield form needs `-o CHANGELOG.md` (write to a fresh outfile) or stdout-redirect, NOT `-i CHANGELOG.md -s` (which is the append mode Story 3.1 uses against an existing seed). Confirm flag semantics before invocation in Task 4
33	  - [x] `git log v0.10.0..HEAD --oneline | wc -l` — record the count of un-tagged commits since the most recent release. The seed run with `-r 0` will emit an `## Unreleased` section (or omit it if zero commits since last tag) — count expectation feeds AC #3 verification
34	
35	- [x] **Task 2 — Reconciliation decision (Story 2.2's owner choice)** (AC #1, AC #4)
36	  - [x] Read Dev Notes § "CHANGELOG reconciliation decision" in this story file in full — three paths are enumerated verbatim per the epics.md § Story 2.2 header carry-forward, plus a recommended path (Path 3) with reasoning
37	  - [x] Pick one of the three paths. The recommendation is **Path 3** (preserve existing 1.0.0 prose to a workshop file, delete `CHANGELOG.md`, seed greenfield from tags, defer hand-curated v1.0.0 entry to Story 5.1's pre-flight). If choosing Path 1 or Path 2, capture the rationale in this story's Dev Agent Record § "Completion Notes List" before any mutation — divergence from the recommendation is allowed but MUST be justified in writing for traceability into Story 3.1 / 5.1
38	  - [x] Whichever path is chosen, the commit body (AC #9 (i)) MUST name it explicitly so future readers can trace the decision
39	
40	- [x] **Task 3 — Preserve existing hand-written v1.0.0 prose** (AC #4) [if Path 3 chosen]
41	  - [x] `mkdir -p _bmad-output/planning-artifacts` (idempotent — directory already exists per planning-artifacts layout)
42	  - [x] Copy the entire current `CHANGELOG.md` contents into `_bmad-output/planning-artifacts/v1-0-0-changelog-draft.md`, prepending a header block that explains: (a) what this file is — the hand-written 2026-04-13 v1.0.0 draft preserved during Story 2.2's CHANGELOG seed, (b) why it's here — Story 5.1's pre-flight audit will pull from it when authoring the actual v1.0.0 CHANGELOG entry, (c) the source — moved from `CHANGELOG.md` repo root by Story 2.2's reconciliation Path 3
43	  - [x] Verify the workshop file's content matches the original byte-for-byte except for the prepended header: `diff <(tail -n +N _bmad-output/planning-artifacts/v1-0-0-changelog-draft.md) CHANGELOG.md` (where N is the line count of the prepended header + 1) should produce no output
44	  - [x] [Path 1 alternative] If Path 1 chosen instead, rename `CHANGELOG.md` → `CHANGELOG.draft.md` (or fold into a section in `docs/STABILITY.md` if Story 2.3 has authored it — but it hasn't yet, so this sub-option is not currently viable)
45	  - [x] [Path 2 alternative] If Path 2 chosen instead, skip this task entirely and skip Task 4's deletion sub-step. Task 5's invocation must then be `-i CHANGELOG.md -s -r 0` (append-mode-against-existing) and the dev agent MUST verify by inspection that the existing `[1.0.0]` heading does not cause the tool to silently skip generating entries for tags older than 1.0.0
46	
47	- [x] **Task 4 — Clear the way for greenfield seed** (AC #1) [if Path 3 chosen]
48	  - [x] `rm CHANGELOG.md` — staged via Task 7's `git add CHANGELOG.md` (which will register a delete + re-add as a single replacement in the commit)
49	  - [x] Confirm `ls CHANGELOG.md 2>&1` reports "No such file" before Task 5 runs (Task 5's `-o CHANGELOG.md` form expects a non-existent target file in a clean greenfield setup)
50	
51	- [x] **Task 5 — Run the seed** (AC #1, AC #2, AC #3)
52	  - [x] Invocation: `npx conventional-changelog-cli -p conventionalcommits -r 0 -o CHANGELOG.md`. Exact rationale for each flag:
53	    - `-p conventionalcommits` — the modern preset (transitively resolved via Story 2.1's `conventional-changelog@^6.0.0`); produces Keep-a-Changelog category headings (Added/Changed/Fixed/etc.) keyed off conventional-commit type prefixes (feat/fix/perf/refactor/etc.)
54	    - `-r 0` — emit one section per release-tag in history; `0` means "all releases" (default is `1` which emits only the latest). Mandatory per AC #1
55	    - `-o CHANGELOG.md` — write to a new outfile. Use this form (not `-i ... -s`) when seeding greenfield. `-i CHANGELOG.md -s` is the append-mode invocation Story 3.1 will use AGAINST the seeded file, NOT for the seed itself
56	  - [x] Capture stdout/stderr to a transient log: `npx conventional-changelog-cli -p conventionalcommits -r 0 -o CHANGELOG.md 2>&1 | tee /tmp/skf-changelog-seed.log`. The log will show the deprecation warning (expected per AC #10) and any preset-loading or git-walking errors. Discard the log after committing the file (do not commit `/tmp` content)
57	  - [x] `wc -l CHANGELOG.md` — sanity check the seed produced something. Expect dozens-to-hundreds of lines (16 tag sections × variable per-tag commit counts, plus preamble). An output under ~30 lines indicates the preset failed to walk history — re-investigate before proceeding
58	  - [x] `grep -c "^## " CHANGELOG.md` — count level-2 headings. Expect 16 (one per tag from v0.3.0 through v0.10.0) plus 0-or-1 for an `## [Unreleased]` header. If significantly fewer, the `-r 0` flag did not take — re-check the invocation
59	
60	- [x] **Task 6 — Hand-review and clean up** (AC #2, AC #5, AC #7)
61	  - [x] Open the seeded `CHANGELOG.md` and scan top-to-bottom. Expected issues to look for and resolve:
62	    - **Missing top-level preamble**: the conventionalcommits preset typically emits only `# Changelog` and the version sections. Add the standard Keep-a-Changelog preamble line ("All notable changes to this project will be documented in this file. The format is based on [Keep a Changelog](https://keepachangelog.com/en/1.1.0/), and this project adheres to [Semantic Versioning](https://semver.org/spec/v2.0.0.html).") below the `# Changelog` heading and before the first `## [...]` section. Match the style the existing 2026-04-13 hand-written file used (per the 3148-byte preserved copy)
63	    - **Misclassified commits**: pre-v0.6.0 commits may have non-conventional prefixes ("Add Entire CLI setup", "0.3.0", "Initial commit"); the preset will either skip them entirely or lump them under "Other". If a tag's section is empty (just a date heading with no entries), that's expected for early tags and acceptable — leave the empty section to preserve the version chronology. AC #2 explicitly permits hand-editing
64	    - **Compare-link footers**: the preset emits `[unreleased]: https://...` and `[X.Y.Z]: https://...` link-reference footers at the bottom. Verify they target `armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge` (not a fallback URL pulled from `package.json`'s `repository` field if it differs). The `repository.url` in `package.json` is `git+https://github.com/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge.git` — the preset SHOULD normalize this correctly, but verify the first compare link by hand
65	    - **Date format consistency**: each `## [X.Y.Z] - YYYY-MM-DD` heading uses the tag's commit date. Spot-check three dates against `git log -1 --format=%ad --date=short v<X.Y.Z>` to confirm
66	  - [x] If hand-edits are made, capture the edit list in Completion Notes (file:line + rationale). AC #2's intent is that subsequent Story 3.1 runs will not overwrite these edits — verifying that intent is Story 3.2's alpha-cut job, not this story's
67	  - [x] Run `npm run lint:md` — confirm `CHANGELOG.md` passes markdownlint. If it doesn't, see Dev Notes § "Markdownlint regression surface" for the expected fix patterns. Do NOT add `CHANGELOG.md` to a markdownlint ignore list — fix the file or extend a rule scoped to Keep-a-Changelog conventions
68	
69	- [x] **Task 7 — Add README link** (AC #6)
70	  - [x] Open `README.md`. Locate the existing `## Release Process` section (line 178). Insert a new `## Changelog` section directly above it (line 178 → becomes a Changelog section heading, then the existing Release Process drops to ~line 182): `## Changelog\n\nPast releases are documented in [CHANGELOG.md](CHANGELOG.md).\n\n` — three lines plus blank-line padding for markdownlint
71	  - [x] Verify the README's existing "Learn More" / "Acknowledgements" / "Release Process" / "License" / "Contributing" ordering is preserved. The new section sits between Contributing (line 174) and Release Process (line 178) by section-flow logic (changelog is reader-facing project history; release process is maintainer-facing operational doc)
72	  - [x] Run `npm run lint:md` again — confirm `README.md` still passes after the insertion. Expected to pass since the format mirrors the surrounding sections
73	
74	- [x] **Task 8 — Full quality suite regression gate** (AC #7)
75	  - [x] Run `npm test`. Expect all 12 subcommands green. Particularly:

93	### What this story actually is
94	
95	This is a **one-shot seed of `CHANGELOG.md` from git tag history** — a single `npx conventional-changelog-cli` invocation, a hand-review pass, and a one-link addition to `README.md`. The repo diff is two-to-four files: `CHANGELOG.md` (replaced from greenfield), `README.md` (+3 lines), `_bmad-output/planning-artifacts/v1-0-0-changelog-draft.md` (new workshop file preserving the existing 1.0.0 prose for Story 5.1), and optionally one markdownlint config file (only if the seeded file's MD024-duplicate-heading pattern requires a narrowed config). No tooling, no scripts, no workflows, no STABILITY.md.
96	
97	The work product is a real, repo-resident, Keep-a-Changelog-shaped historical record from `v0.3.0` through `v0.10.0` that downstream consumers can audit without leaving the repo, and that Story 3.1's `release.yaml` can append to via `npx conventional-changelog-cli -i CHANGELOG.md -s` on every future release.
98	
99	**Scope discipline — what NOT to do here:**
100	
101	- Do NOT preserve the pre-existing hand-written `## [1.0.0] - 2026-04-13` entry inside the seeded `CHANGELOG.md`. It pre-announces a version that has not been released (current published version is `0.10.0`). Letting it survive into the seeded file means downstream readers see a "v1.0.0 — released 2026-04-13" entry that contradicts npm. The prose ITSELF is valuable raw material for Story 5.1's eventual hand-curated v1.0.0 entry — preserve it to a workshop file, not into the published changelog.
102	- Do NOT author `docs/STABILITY.md`. That is Story 2.3's scope. Story 2.2 ships changelog history; STABILITY.md ships the v1.0.0 API contract. Two orthogonal artifacts.
103	- Do NOT extend `docs/RELEASING.md` with rollback playbook content. That is Story 4.1's scope. The existing `<!-- Rollback Playbook — added in Story 4.1 -->` placeholder in `docs/RELEASING.md` (per Epic 1 retro § "Patterns across all three stories") MUST stay intact.
104	- Do NOT add `release:changelog` or any `release:*` npm scripts to `package.json`. Story 4.2 will deprecate the existing `release:*` scripts; Story 6.2 will delete them. Adding new ones now creates a second canonical path that the deprecation/deletion stories then have to retire — exactly the trap Story 2.1's scope discipline (per its Dev Notes § "Scope discipline — what NOT to do here") preempted.
105	- Do NOT switch tools to silence the `conventional-changelog-cli@5.0.0` deprecation warning. The choice between staying on the deprecated-but-working `conventional-changelog-cli@5` versus migrating to the suggested `conventional-changelog` package versus the rejected-but-still-viable `git-cliff` is captured in `_bmad-output/implementation-artifacts/deferred-work.md` § "Story 3.1 tooling decision". Forcing the decision here pre-empts Story 3.1's design phase.
106	- Do NOT bump `conventional-changelog-cli` past `^5.0.0` in `package.json`. Story 2.1 deliberately pinned `^5.0.0`. Bumping requires a separate deliberate change with its own `npm test` regression gate.
107	- Do NOT add `CHANGELOG.md` to a markdownlint ignore list as a shortcut around MD024 (duplicate-heading). The right fix is a narrow rule config (sibling-only duplicates allowed) or a CHANGELOG-scoped override — not exempting the file from quality gates.
108	- Do NOT manually copy commit text into the seeded file. The whole point of the tool is deterministic categorization; hand-fabricating entries defeats the audit-trail-from-git semantic Story 3.1 will rely on. Hand EDITS for noise correction are fine (AC #2); hand AUTHORING new entries is not.

144	### CHANGELOG reconciliation decision
145	
146	Per the epics.md Story 2.2 § header (lines 291-297), **three paths are available** for resolving the conflict between the original AC #1's "Given no `CHANGELOG.md` exists" framing and the live-state reality (a hand-written 2026-04-13 v1.0.0 draft already at the repo root). Story 2.1's Dev Notes § "Existing CHANGELOG.md — flag for Story 2.2" is the verbatim source for these three paths. **Decision is Story 2.2's owner to make**, but the reasoning below makes Path 3 the strongly recommended default:
147	
148	**Path 1 — Rename existing file, seed greenfield, hand-merge prose later**
149	
150	- Operation: `git mv CHANGELOG.md CHANGELOG.draft.md` (or fold into `docs/STABILITY.md`'s launch section), then `npx conventional-changelog-cli -p conventionalcommits -r 0 -o CHANGELOG.md`, then hand-merge the 1.0.0 prose into the seeded file's top entry
151	- Tradeoffs:
152	  - PRO: Preserves the prose file in-tree where reviewers see it
153	  - CON: `CHANGELOG.draft.md` is non-standard naming (downstream tools / IDEs that auto-detect changelogs by canonical filename will not find it). `docs/STABILITY.md` does not yet exist (Story 2.3's job) and folding the changelog prose into a file about API contracts mixes two orthogonal concerns
154	  - CON: Hand-merging the 1.0.0 prose into the seeded file's top entry presumes 1.0.0 is the latest version — which it isn't. 1.0.0 has not been released
155	
156	**Path 2 — Keep existing file, run `-i CHANGELOG.md -s` in append mode**
157	
158	- Operation: `npx conventional-changelog-cli -p conventionalcommits -i CHANGELOG.md -s -r 0` against the existing 3148-byte file
159	- Tradeoffs:
160	  - PRO: Single-file diff (only `CHANGELOG.md` changes); no workshop file needed
161	  - CON: `-i ... -s` semantics: the tool reads the existing file, finds the most recent version heading (`## [1.0.0] - 2026-04-13`), and inserts new entries ABOVE that heading. With `-r 0`, it would walk all releases, but the existing `[1.0.0]` heading is in the way — the tool's "stop at the most recent existing version" heuristic may either (a) skip emitting any sections older than 1.0.0 (because 1.0.0 is the apparent boundary), (b) emit them above 1.0.0 producing a chronologically-inverted file, or (c) just refuse to walk past 1.0.0. Behavior is uncertain enough that this path requires a probe-then-decide loop the dev agent has not budgeted for
162	  - CON: Even if the tool produces correct output, the resulting file still contains the pre-emptive `[1.0.0]` entry that contradicts npm reality. AC #2 / AC #4 then have nowhere to land cleanly
163	
164	**Path 3 — Delete pre-emptive `[1.0.0]` entry, seed from tags, defer hand-curated 1.0.0 to Story 5.1 (RECOMMENDED)**
165	
166	- Operation: copy current CHANGELOG.md to `_bmad-output/planning-artifacts/v1-0-0-changelog-draft.md` (workshop file with header), `rm CHANGELOG.md`, run `npx conventional-changelog-cli -p conventionalcommits -r 0 -o CHANGELOG.md` against an empty target, hand-review for noise, commit
167	- Tradeoffs:
168	  - PRO: Aligns with epics.md Story 5.1 AC ("`CHANGELOG.md` exists in repo with a hand-curated v1.0.0 entry added at the top (not just auto-generated; curator's judgment on wording, not commit grep)") — Story 5.1 explicitly anticipates authoring the v1.0.0 entry as part of pre-flight audit
169	  - PRO: Aligns with original epics.md AC #1's "Given no `CHANGELOG.md` exists" framing (we genuinely delete first, so the file doesn't exist when the seed runs)
170	  - PRO: Workshop file `_bmad-output/planning-artifacts/v1-0-0-changelog-draft.md` is a discoverable artifact that Story 5.1's `create-story` will surface. The 14-workflow table, 23-IDE list, and "Highlights" prose are not lost — just staged for the moment they actually correspond to a real published version
171	  - PRO: Resulting `CHANGELOG.md` is unambiguous — every entry corresponds to a real published tag on npm
172	  - CON: Two-file diff (CHANGELOG.md + workshop file) instead of one. Minor cost
173	  - CON: Story 5.1's dev agent must remember to pull from the workshop file. Mitigated by referencing it explicitly in the workshop file's header AND in this story's Completion Notes (so when create-story runs for Story 5.1, the workshop file's existence is in the immediately-prior story's Dev Notes)
174	
175	**Recommendation: Path 3.** It cleanly resolves the AC contradiction, preserves the 1.0.0 prose for Story 5.1's planned use, and produces a published `CHANGELOG.md` whose every entry corresponds to a real npm-published version.
176	
177	If choosing Path 1 or Path 2 instead, the Completion Notes MUST capture the rationale and the Tasks section MUST be adapted (Path 1 swaps Task 4's `rm` for `git mv`; Path 2 skips Tasks 3-4 entirely and reshapes Task 5's invocation to `-i ... -s`).
178	
179	### Tool flag reference — for the dev agent
180	
181	`conventional-changelog-cli` flag semantics (per Story 2.1 verification + the tool's `--help` surface):
182	
183	| Flag | Meaning | Story 2.2 (seed) | Story 3.1 (append) |
184	|------|---------|------------------|---------------------|
185	| `-p <preset>` | Conventional commit preset | `conventionalcommits` | `conventionalcommits` |
186	| `-r <n>` | Release count: `0`=all, `1`=latest only (default) | `0` (per AC #1) | `1` (default; new entry only) |
187	| `-o <file>` | Outfile (write to fresh file) | `CHANGELOG.md` (greenfield) | not used |
188	| `-i <file>` | Infile (read existing) | not used (greenfield) | `CHANGELOG.md` |
189	| `-s` | Same-file (overwrite infile) | not used | yes (paired with `-i`) |
190	
191	**Critical distinction:** `-i CHANGELOG.md -s` is APPEND mode (used by Story 3.1's `release.yaml` for incremental updates). It expects an existing file with prior version headings. Using it for a greenfield seed against a deleted-or-empty file is undefined behavior. Story 2.2's seed MUST use `-o CHANGELOG.md` (write to a fresh outfile) instead.
192	
193	If the dev agent's `--help` output disagrees with this table at execution time (the tool's surface may have shifted between the recon snapshot and execution), trust `--help` over the table and capture the deviation in Completion Notes.
194	
195	### Markdownlint regression surface
196	
197	`markdownlint-cli2` (`npm run lint:md`) walks every `**/*.md` file in the repo per `package.json` line 45. The new `CHANGELOG.md` will be linted; the existing 2026-04-13 hand-written file passed (per Story 2.1 Task 5 green run) but the seeded version's shape will differ. Likely friction points:
198	
199	1. **MD024 (no-duplicate-heading)** — Keep-a-Changelog repeats `### Added`, `### Fixed`, `### Changed` etc. across every version section. Default markdownlint config flags these as duplicates. Standard fix: enable `siblings_only: true` for MD024 (allow duplicate headings if they're under different parent headings — which `### Added` always is, since each appears under a different `## [X.Y.Z]` parent). Check the existing markdownlint config first (`.markdownlint*.json`, `.markdownlint*.yaml`, `package.json` `markdownlint` key) to see if `siblings_only` is already configured. If not, add the narrowest possible config — ideally CHANGELOG-scoped via a `.markdownlint-cli2.yaml` glob override, not a repo-wide change
200	
201	2. **MD013 (line-length)** — long URLs in compare-link footers (`[v0.10.0]: https://github.com/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/compare/v0.9.0...v0.10.0`) may exceed 120-char default. Fix: scoped override allowing long lines in the link-reference footer area, or globally `tables: false, code_blocks: false` if not already set
202	
203	3. **MD041 (first-line-h1)** — should pass; the conventionalcommits preset emits `# Changelog` as the first heading
204	
205	4. **MD025 (single-h1)** — should pass; only one `# Changelog` heading, all version sections are `## [X.Y.Z]`
206	
207	5. **MD012 (no-multiple-blanks)** — preset output may have trailing/leading blanks between sections. Hand-edit if flagged
208	
209	If any rule trips, prefer narrowing the rule's scope over disabling it. Adding `CHANGELOG.md` to an `ignores` list is forbidden (per Dev Notes § "Scope discipline" above) — quality gates apply to repo-resident files.

file.content:
# Story 2.2: Seed initial `CHANGELOG.md` from existing git tags

Status: review

<!-- Note: Validation is optional. Run validate-create-story for quality check before dev-story. -->

## Story

As a downstream user pinning Skill Forge as a dependency,
I want a repo-resident `CHANGELOG.md` following Keep-a-Changelog v1.1.0 format with one versioned entry per published tag from `v0.3.0` through `v0.10.0`,
so that I can audit what changed in each past version without leaving the repo, the v1.0.0 release has a pre-existing changelog history to append to, and Story 3.1's `release.yaml` has a real seed file against which `npx conventional-changelog-cli -p conventionalcommits -i CHANGELOG.md -s` can append the next release entry.

## Acceptance Criteria

1. The CHANGELOG seed SHALL be produced from `npx conventional-changelog-cli -p conventionalcommits -r 0` (all-releases mode) — see Dev Notes § "CHANGELOG reconciliation decision" for the three reconciliation paths Story 2.1 flagged and the pre-condition that resolves the "Given no `CHANGELOG.md` exists" framing of the original AC.
2. The resulting `CHANGELOG.md` SHALL exist at the repo root and SHALL follow Keep-a-Changelog v1.1.0 shape: a top-level `# Changelog` header, an "All notable changes…" preamble line, then one `## [X.Y.Z] - YYYY-MM-DD` versioned section per published tag from `v0.3.0` through `v0.10.0` (16 sections total — see Dev Notes § "Live-state recon" for the exact tag list), each containing one or more of the Keep-a-Changelog category headings (`### Added`, `### Changed`, `### Deprecated`, `### Removed`, `### Fixed`, `### Security`) as the conventionalcommits preset emits them.
3. Tags newer than the most recent published version (none currently — `v0.10.0` is HEAD's most recent tag) SHALL NOT have a section. An "Unreleased" section MAY appear at the top if the tool emits one for commits since `v0.10.0`; if it does, the `Unreleased` heading SHALL be left in place (it's the standard Keep-a-Changelog hook for accumulating un-tagged work; Story 3.1 will replace it on the next release cut).
4. The pre-existing hand-written `## [1.0.0] - 2026-04-13` prose at the repo root SHALL NOT survive into the seeded `CHANGELOG.md`. The prose SHALL be preserved at `_bmad-output/planning-artifacts/v1-0-0-changelog-draft.md` (workshop file) so Story 5.1's pre-flight audit can pull the curated wording back when v1.0.0 is actually being cut. The workshop file SHALL include a one-line header explaining its purpose and pointing to Story 5.1 as the consumer.
5. The seed output SHALL be hand-reviewed for noise (commits incorrectly classified by the conventionalcommits preset, missing scopes, accidentally-shipped sentinel commits) and any corrections SHALL be applied directly to the file. AC #2 of the original epics.md formulation ("subsequent Epic 3 runs SHALL append new entries without overwriting existing prose") is satisfied by `conventional-changelog-cli -i ... -s`'s "stop at the first existing version heading" behavior — Story 3.1's append invocation will not touch the seeded prose, so hand-edits stick.
6. `README.md` SHALL gain a "Changelog" link pointing to `CHANGELOG.md`, placed adjacent to the existing "Release Process" subsection (lines 178-180 of README.md). The link SHALL be a one-sentence pointer ("Past releases are documented in [CHANGELOG.md](CHANGELOG.md).") — not a badge, not a duplicated table of versions. Discoverability is the goal; not duplicating the changelog into the README.
7. `npm test` (the full quality suite — `test:schemas`, `test:install`, `test:cli`, `test:workflow`, `test:python`, `test:knowledge`, `validate:schemas`, `validate:skills`, `validate:refs`, `lint`, `lint:md`, `format:check`) SHALL pass on the resulting branch. In particular `lint:md` SHALL pass on the new `CHANGELOG.md` file — see Dev Notes § "Markdownlint regression surface" for likely fix patterns if the seeded output trips a rule.
8. No file outside the seven below SHALL be modified by this story: `CHANGELOG.md` (replaced), `README.md` (one-link addition), `_bmad-output/planning-artifacts/v1-0-0-changelog-draft.md` (new workshop file). No changes to `package.json`, `package-lock.json`, `.github/workflows/*`, `docs/RELEASING.md`, `docs/STABILITY.md` (not yet authored — Story 2.3), or any source/test file. If the seeded file requires a markdownlint config tweak (e.g., extending an `ignores`/`MD024` rule to handle Keep-a-Changelog's repeated category headings), that tweak IS in scope and the touched config file is the eighth allowed file — see Dev Notes § "Markdownlint regression surface" for the expected fix path.
9. The commit SHALL use conventional-commit form. Suggested subject: `docs(changelog): seed initial CHANGELOG.md from existing tags v0.3.0..v0.10.0`. The body SHALL record (i) the reconciliation path chosen (recommended: Path 3 per Dev Notes § "CHANGELOG reconciliation decision") and the rationale, (ii) the exact `conventional-changelog-cli` invocation used (flags + version), (iii) one-line confirmation `npm test` passed post-seed, (iv) one-line note that the hand-written `[1.0.0]` prose was preserved to `_bmad-output/planning-artifacts/v1-0-0-changelog-draft.md` for Story 5.1, (v) one-line restatement that no `package.json` / `package-lock.json` / workflow changes piggybacked.
10. The deprecation warning Story 2.1 surfaced (`npm warn deprecated conventional-changelog-cli@5.0.0: This package is no longer maintained.`) SHALL appear during this story's seed invocation as well — that is expected and NOT a blocker. The warning is registry-side; tool execution remains correct. Do NOT switch tools to silence it (the choice between staying on `conventional-changelog-cli@5` versus migrating to `conventional-changelog` versus `git-cliff` is a Story 3.1 design-phase decision, captured in `_bmad-output/implementation-artifacts/deferred-work.md` § "Story 3.1 tooling decision").

## Tasks / Subtasks

- [x] **Task 1 — Live-state recon** (Epic 1 retrospective carry-forward; informs every later task)
  - [x] `git tag -l --sort=-creatordate` — confirm 16 tags `v0.10.0` → `v0.3.0` exist (no v0.1.x, no v0.2.x). If the count or shape differs from the recon snapshot in Dev Notes § "Live-state recon", capture the live state in Completion Notes and adjust expectations
  - [x] `wc -c CHANGELOG.md` and `git log -1 --format='%H %ad' -- CHANGELOG.md` — confirm the file is still the 3148-byte hand-written 2026-04-13 v1.0.0 draft Story 2.1 left untouched. If a different shape is on disk (e.g. someone else seeded between Story 2.1 and now), STOP and reconcile out-of-band before proceeding
  - [x] `npx --no-install conventional-changelog-cli --version` — confirm the binary resolves locally (Story 2.1's lockfile carries `conventional-changelog-cli@5.0.0`); expected output `5.0.0`
  - [x] `npx --no-install conventional-changelog-cli --help | head -40` — re-read the flag surface fresh. AC #1 mandates `-r 0`; the greenfield form needs `-o CHANGELOG.md` (write to a fresh outfile) or stdout-redirect, NOT `-i CHANGELOG.md -s` (which is the append mode Story 3.1 uses against an existing seed). Confirm flag semantics before invocation in Task 4
  - [x] `git log v0.10.0..HEAD --oneline | wc -l` — record the count of un-tagged commits since the most recent release. The seed run with `-r 0` will emit an `## Unreleased` section (or omit it if zero commits since last tag) — count expectation feeds AC #3 verification

- [x] **Task 2 — Reconciliation decision (Story 2.2's owner choice)** (AC #1, AC #4)
  - [x] Read Dev Notes § "CHANGELOG reconciliation decision" in this story file in full — three paths are enumerated verbatim per the epics.md § Story 2.2 header carry-forward, plus a recommended path (Path 3) with reasoning
  - [x] Pick one of the three paths. The recommendation is **Path 3** (preserve existing 1.0.0 prose to a workshop file, delete `CHANGELOG.md`, seed greenfield from tags, defer hand-curated v1.0.0 entry to Story 5.1's pre-flight). If choosing Path 1 or Path 2, capture the rationale in this story's Dev Agent Record § "Completion Notes List" before any mutation — divergence from the recommendation is allowed but MUST be justified in writing for traceability into Story 3.1 / 5.1
  - [x] Whichever path is chosen, the commit body (AC #9 (i)) MUST name it explicitly so future readers can trace the decision

- [x] **Task 3 — Preserve existing hand-written v1.0.0 prose** (AC #4) [if Path 3 chosen]
  - [x] `mkdir -p _bmad-output/planning-artifacts` (idempotent — directory already exists per planning-artifacts layout)
  - [x] Copy the entire current `CHANGELOG.md` contents into `_bmad-output/planning-artifacts/v1-0-0-changelog-draft.md`, prepending a header block that explains: (a) what this file is — the hand-written 2026-04-13 v1.0.0 draft preserved during Story 2.2's CHANGELOG seed, (b) why it's here — Story 5.1's pre-flight audit will pull from it when authoring the actual v1.0.0 CHANGELOG entry, (c) the source — moved from `CHANGELOG.md` repo root by Story 2.2's reconciliation Path 3
  - [x] Verify the workshop file's content matches the original byte-for-byte except for the prepended header: `diff <(tail -n +N _bmad-output/planning-artifacts/v1-0-0-changelog-draft.md) CHANGELOG.md` (where N is the line count of the prepended header + 1) should produce no output
  - [x] [Path 1 alternative] If Path 1 chosen instead, rename `CHANGELOG.md` → `CHANGELOG.draft.md` (or fold into a section in `docs/STABILITY.md` if Story 2.3 has authored it — but it hasn't yet, so this sub-option is not currently viable)
  - [x] [Path 2 alternative] If Path 2 chosen instead, skip this task entirely and skip Task 4's deletion sub-step. Task 5's invocation must then be `-i CHANGELOG.md -s -r 0` (append-mode-against-existing) and the dev agent MUST verify by inspection that the existing `[1.0.0]` heading does not cause the tool to silently skip generating entries for tags older than 1.0.0

- [x] **Task 4 — Clear the way for greenfield seed** (AC #1) [if Path 3 chosen]
  - [x] `rm CHANGELOG.md` — staged via Task 7's `git add CHANGELOG.md` (which will register a delete + re-add as a single replacement in the commit)
  - [x] Confirm `ls CHANGELOG.md 2>&1` reports "No such file" before Task 5 runs (Task 5's `-o CHANGELOG.md` form expects a non-existent target file in a clean greenfield setup)

- [x] **Task 5 — Run the seed** (AC #1, AC #2, AC #3)
  - [x] Invocation: `npx conventional-changelog-cli -p conventionalcommits -r 0 -o CHANGELOG.md`. Exact rationale for each flag:
    - `-p conventionalcommits` — the modern preset (transitively resolved via Story 2.1's `conventional-changelog@^6.0.0`); produces Keep-a-Changelog category headings (Added/Changed/Fixed/etc.) keyed off conventional-commit type prefixes (feat/fix/perf/refactor/etc.)
    - `-r 0` — emit one section per release-tag in history; `0` means "all releases" (default is `1` which emits only the latest). Mandatory per AC #1
    - `-o CHANGELOG.md` — write to a new outfile. Use this form (not `-i ... -s`) when seeding greenfield. `-i CHANGELOG.md -s` is the append-mode invocation Story 3.1 will use AGAINST the seeded file, NOT for the seed itself

## Dev Notes

### What this story actually is

This is a **one-shot seed of `CHANGELOG.md` from git tag history** — a single `npx conventional-changelog-cli` invocation, a hand-review pass, and a one-link addition to `README.md`. The repo diff is two-to-four files: `CHANGELOG.md` (replaced from greenfield), `README.md` (+3 lines), `_bmad-output/planning-artifacts/v1-0-0-changelog-draft.md` (new workshop file preserving the existing 1.0.0 prose for Story 5.1), and optionally one markdownlint config file (only if the seeded file's MD024-duplicate-heading pattern requires a narrowed config). No tooling, no scripts, no workflows, no STABILITY.md.

The work product is a real, repo-resident, Keep-a-Changelog-shaped historical record from `v0.3.0` through `v0.10.0` that downstream consumers can audit without leaving the repo, and that Story 3.1's `release.yaml` can append to via `npx conventional-changelog-cli -i CHANGELOG.md -s` on every future release.

**Scope discipline — what NOT to do here:**

- Do NOT preserve the pre-existing hand-written `## [1.0.0] - 2026-04-13` entry inside the seeded `CHANGELOG.md`. It pre-announces a version that has not been released (current published version is `0.10.0`). Letting it survive into the seeded file means downstream readers see a "v1.0.0 — released 2026-04-13" entry that contradicts npm. The prose ITSELF is valuable raw material for Story 5.1's eventual hand-curated v1.0.0 entry — preserve it to a workshop file, not into the published changelog.
- Do NOT author `docs/STABILITY.md`. That is Story 2.3's scope. Story 2.2 ships changelog history; STABILITY.md ships the v1.0.0 API contract. Two orthogonal artifacts.
- Do NOT extend `docs/RELEASING.md` with rollback playbook content. That is Story 4.1's scope. The existing `<!-- Rollback Playbook — added in Story 4.1 -->` placeholder in `docs/RELEASING.md` (per Epic 1 retro § "Patterns across all three stories") MUST stay intact.
- Do NOT add `release:changelog` or any `release:*` npm scripts to `package.json`. Story 4.2 will deprecate the existing `release:*` scripts; Story 6.2 will delete them. Adding new ones now creates a second canonical path that the deprecation/deletion stories then have to retire — exactly the trap Story 2.1's scope discipline (per its Dev Notes § "Scope discipline — what NOT to do here") preempted.
- Do NOT switch tools to silence the `conventional-changelog-cli@5.0.0` deprecation warning. The choice between staying on the deprecated-but-working `conventional-changelog-cli@5` versus migrating to the suggested `conventional-changelog` package versus the rejected-but-still-viable `git-cliff` is captured in `_bmad-output/implementation-artifacts/deferred-work.md` § "Story 3.1 tooling decision". Forcing the decision here pre-empts Story 3.1's design phase.
- Do NOT bump `conventional-changelog-cli` past `^5.0.0` in `package.json`. Story 2.1 deliberately pinned `^5.0.0`. Bumping requires a separate deliberate change with its own `npm test` regression gate.
- Do NOT add `CHANGELOG.md` to a markdownlint ignore list as a shortcut around MD024 (duplicate-heading). The right fix is a narrow rule config (sibling-only duplicates allowed) or a CHANGELOG-scoped override — not exempting the file from quality gates.
- Do NOT manually copy commit text into the seeded file. The whole point of the tool is deterministic categorization; hand-fabricating entries defeats the audit-trail-from-git semantic Story 3.1 will rely on. Hand EDITS for noise correction are fine (AC #2); hand AUTHORING new entries is not.
